faq.answer: Send a short message to attendees explaining what the meeting accomplished, why you are ending it, and what replaces it going forward — a written update, a different cadence, a Slack channel, or nothing at all. This frames the cancellation as a purposeful transition rather than abandonment. Give a few days' notice if possible.

faq.question: What should replace a cancelled recurring meeting?
faq.answer: The replacement depends on what need the meeting was serving. If it was a status update, a shared written document or async message thread often works better. If it was a decision-making forum, an ad-hoc meeting called only when decisions are needed is more efficient. If it was purely habitual with no clear purpose, nothing replaces it.

faq.question: How do I suggest cancelling a meeting I don't own?
faq.answer: Approach the organizer privately and frame it as a question rather than a suggestion: 'I wanted to check in about what value the meeting is providing for you — I'd be glad to discuss whether a different format might work better.' This opens a conversation without challenging the organizer's judgment and gives them the opportunity to reflect on the meeting's purpose.

faq.question: Can recurring meetings ever be redesigned instead of cancelled?
faq.answer: Yes, and this is often a better outcome than cancellation. A weekly one-hour meeting that has drifted might be better as a monthly 30-minute check-in, or a bi-weekly 15-minute standup. Changing the frequency, duration, or format can restore the meeting's usefulness without eliminating the cadence entirely. The audit process surfaces these options.

medium_body:
Recurring meetings are the most overlooked category on most knowledge workers' calendars. They were created at a specific moment, for a specific purpose, and then continued automatically long after that purpose was resolved or changed.

The mechanism is simple: creating a recurring meeting takes one action. Canceling it requires noticing it has become unnecessary, which requires stepping back from the routine long enough to evaluate it. Most people never do. The meeting runs until someone leaves the team or the calendar gets audited.

A recurring meeting audit takes about twenty minutes and often recovers hours per week. The process is: list every recurring meeting you attend or own, and for each one ask three questions. What was the original purpose? Is that purpose still active? What would actually break if this meeting stopped tomorrow?

The third question is the most revealing. For many recurring meetings, the honest answer is 'nothing.' The information shared in the meeting could flow through a written update. The alignment achieved could happen through a shared document. The decisions made are so infrequent they could happen in an ad-hoc call when genuinely needed.

For meetings you own, you have direct authority to cancel or reshape them. A short message to attendees — what the meeting accomplished, why it is ending, and what replaces it — turns the cancellation into a transition rather than an abandonment.

For meetings you attend but do not own, a private conversation with the organizer works better than raising the topic in the meeting itself. Framing it as curiosity rather than critique — 'I wanted to check in about what this meeting is currently providing' — opens the door to a productive redesign without putting the organizer on the defensive.

Build this audit into your quarterly planning. It takes less time than any individual meeting it eliminates, and the dividends compound every week afterward.

For related reading, see our guide on meeting preparation checklists for the recurring meetings that survive the audit.

meta_description: Why buffer time between meetings is not optional. How meeting gaps improve focus, reduce stress, and make every meeting more effective.
intro: Buffer time between meetings is not a luxury or a sign of an underbooked calendar. It is the minimum condition for showing up to each meeting fully present.

body_sections.heading: What Happens Without Buffer Time
body_sections.content:
When meetings are stacked end-to-end, several things happen simultaneously. Attendees arrive late to the second meeting because the first ran long. People enter the next call still mentally processing the previous one — attention residue that degrades the quality of listening and contributing. No one has time to write the follow-up from the first meeting before the next one starts.

Over a full day of back-to-back meetings, these costs compound. By late afternoon, decision quality drops, patience thins, and the backlog of unprocessed meeting outputs creates stress that persists into the evening.

body_sections.heading: What Buffer Time Actually Provides
body_sections.content:
A five-minute gap between meetings is enough to close one context and prepare for the next — if you use it deliberately. A ten-minute gap allows you to write a quick recap of what was decided, use the bathroom, drink water, and arrive at the next meeting one minute early.

Fifteen minutes between a complex meeting and the next one is often enough to process, note key actions, and genuinely reset. This is not wasted calendar time — it is the cost of attending meetings effectively rather than just attending them.

Buffer time is not blank space. It is transition time. The difference between a 30-minute meeting and a 25-minute meeting plus a 5-minute transition is the difference between a meeting and an effective meeting.

body_sections.heading: How to Build Buffer Time Into Your Schedule
bullets: Change your calendar default to 25-minute and 50-minute meetings. This creates gaps automatically.
Block a 10-minute 'transition' event after any meeting that is known to be emotionally demanding.
Communicate your scheduling preferences to frequent collaborators so they use the shorter defaults too.
On heavy meeting days, protect at least one 30-minute break in the middle of the day.
If someone schedules a back-to-back, propose a 5-minute adjustment rather than accepting it.

body_sections.heading: Buffer Time as a Signal of Meeting Culture
body_sections.content:
Teams where everyone routinely schedules back-to-back meetings are signaling something: meetings are treated as the primary work rather than as a tool for enabling work. Organizations that protect transition time tend to have meetings with better preparation, clearer decisions, and better follow-through — because their people are not perpetually exhausted and behind.

For more on building healthier meeting habits, see our guide on meeting hygiene best practices.

body_sections.heading: How Schedule Calendar helps
body_sections.content: Schedule Calendar shows your next meeting and how long until it starts, directly from the browser toolbar. During a buffer period, glancing at the popup tells you exactly how many minutes you have before the next commitment — without opening a new tab. This makes the transition window feel real and bounded, rather than a vague space you might accidentally extend or lose track of.

sidebar_takeaways: 5 minutes between meetings is the minimum — 10 is better.
Change default meeting durations to create gaps automatically.
Use transition time to write recaps and reset mentally.
Back-to-back meetings degrade attention quality throughout the day.
sidebar_cta_text: Track your next meeting's countdown from the toolbar. Schedule Calendar for Chrome.
related_title: Related: How to Recover From Back-to-Back Meeting Days

faq.question: How much buffer time should you have between meetings?
faq.answer: A minimum of five minutes is needed for basic transition — walking to another room, finding a link, or closing one context before opening the next. Ten minutes allows for a brief recap note and genuine mental reset. For emotionally demanding meetings or meetings that require complex follow-up, fifteen to twenty minutes is appropriate.

faq.question: How do I create buffer time in my calendar?
faq.answer: The most effective method is changing your calendar tool's default meeting duration from 30 minutes to 25, and from 60 to 50. This creates gaps automatically without requiring anyone else to change their habits. You can also block short 'transition' events after specific meetings you know will need follow-through time.

faq.question: Why are back-to-back meetings problematic?
faq.answer: Back-to-back meetings create attention residue — the previous meeting's unresolved threads remain active in your working memory as you try to engage with the next one. This reduces the quality of listening, contributing, and decision-making across the day. The cognitive switching cost between conversations is also higher than most people expect, especially over a full day of meetings.

faq.question: Is it unprofessional to ask for buffer time between meetings?
faq.answer: No. Asking for five or ten minutes between consecutive meetings is a sign of good self-management, not unavailability. A simple 'I'll be ready at 2:05 rather than 2:00 — does that work?' is usually accepted without friction. People who manage their own transitions well tend to arrive better prepared and contribute more effectively.

faq.question: Can buffer time help reduce meeting-related stress?
faq.answer: Yes, significantly. Much of meeting-related stress comes from the feeling of always being behind — late to the next meeting, without time to capture what was just decided, unable to prepare for the next conversation. Buffer time interrupts that cycle. Even ten minutes between meetings creates a moment of control in an otherwise reactive day.

faq.question: What should I do during buffer time between meetings?
faq.answer: Use the first few minutes to note any decisions or action items from the previous meeting before they fade. Then shift mentally — look at the next meeting's agenda, remember who will be there and what your role is. The last minute or two can be for physical transition: getting water, standing up, moving to a new location. Do not spend buffer time on email or Slack.

medium_body:
The decision to schedule meetings back-to-back is made quickly and costs slowly. The immediate logic is clean: the calendar is full, there is a lot to accomplish, and leaving gaps feels wasteful. The actual cost reveals itself across the day: people arriving late, still mentally in the previous call; decisions made with less care than they deserve; follow-up notes never written because the next meeting started before the last one fully ended.

Buffer time between meetings is not a luxury. It is the minimum condition for attending meetings effectively rather than merely attending them.

Five minutes is enough for the basics — closing one mental context before opening the next, navigating to the correct link, and arriving without the breathless energy of someone who just ran from another call. Ten minutes allows you to jot down the key decisions from the previous meeting before memory decays, drink water, and look at the next meeting's agenda. Fifteen minutes allows genuine reset after something emotionally demanding.

The most practical way to build buffer time is to change your meeting duration defaults. Most calendar tools allow you to set a default meeting length. If yours is 30 minutes, change it to 25. If it is 60, change it to 50. This creates gaps automatically without requiring negotiation on every invite. The five or ten minutes appear because the meeting ends before the next one begins.

A secondary technique for meetings you know will be complex or emotionally loaded: block a short transition event afterward. Mark it as busy. The calendar shows it and people will not schedule into it. You do not need to explain it.

Teams where back-to-back scheduling is the norm are signaling something about how they view meetings — as the primary work rather than a tool for enabling work. Protecting transition time is a small act with an outsize effect on how everyone experiences the day.

For more on building the habits that make meetings worth attending, see our guide on meeting hygiene best practices.
